text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali and I'm going to be posting a few videos. This is called Hindsight is Twenty Twenty. This is a morbidly obese patient with a BMI of sixty five. She presented with obstructive jaundice. CT scan was normal apart from dilated intra and extra hepatic biliary tree and showed some stones in the gallbladder. Her bilirubin was elevated as total I think was like twelve. So I decided to take her to the OR and perform a colostectomy with cholangiogram possible CBD exploration transcystic or colitocotomy.

The surgeon observes an inflamed gallbladder and makes an incision, releasing purulent material. A robotic suction irrigator is used to remove bile. The liver appears large, and white pearl stones are visible within the gallbladder. The incision allows inspection of the cystic duct and common bile duct, which did not light up with ICG, while the liver appears green.

The surgeon attempts to cannulate the cystic duct opening using a wire, but is unsuccessful. A second .035 wire is introduced for cholangiogram purposes, but this attempt also fails. The common bile duct appears visibly dilated and thickened. Indocyanine green (ICG) imaging is used to confirm the absence of a bile leak, although the common bile duct does not light up.

The surgeon attempts to perform a cholangiogram through the common bile duct, using a small catheter. Due to the patient's body habitus, the procedure is challenging. The surgeon carefully avoids the portal vein while inserting the needle, but no bile is aspirated. Upon flushing with saline, blood is observed, indicating the needle has entered the portal vein. A contrast study reveals a portal venogram, prompting the surgeon to remove the needle.

The surgeon opens the common bile duct, revealing a thickened wall with a mucosal lesion resembling a polyp or cancer near the common hepatic duct, above the cystic duct insertion. A scope is passed distally through the common bile duct, showing no intraluminal stones or obvious lesions, and continues through the sphincter of Oddi into the duodenum.

The surgeon carefully retracts the scope from the distal portion of the common bile duct, observing normal appearance, and then advances it proximally to visualize the right and left hepatic ducts. A clot-like structure is visible on the right side, prompting the placement of a seven French, ten centimeter biliary stent.

The surgeon inserts a seven French, ten centimeter biliary stent into the common bile duct, using two graspers to guide it distally into the duodenum. As the stent is passed proximally, bile begins to flow out of the duct. An x-ray confirms the stent's position, with the distal end in the duodenum and the proximal end in the hepatic duct. The surgeon closes the choledochotomy with interrupted sutures using a five-oh PDS and performs brushing of the common bile duct for cytology.

So, um, uh, reason I call hindsight, and, uh, the reason I, uh, uh, um, you know, like number one, uh, having a cystic duct obstructed to that level, chances of you having a stone in the common bile duct is almost nonexisting. Number two, uh, the common bile duct didn't light up with the, uh, ICG. So that should have been a sign that there is a proximal obstruction. Typically the stones, when they do pass, I mean, they're smaller. I mean, they pass through the cystic duct. So they, They do pass a little bit more distally.
